questions.question: Explain Java Server Pages (JSP) and list its advantages over Servlets.
questions.hint: Focus on key advantages: embedded Java, implicit objects, compilation mechanism
keyPoints: JSP is a server-side technology for creating dynamic web pages
Separation of presentation and business logic
Easy to write HTML content
Automatic compilation to servlet
Built-in implicit objects (request, response, session, out, application)
Supports expression language and custom tags
Easier for web designers to work with

questions.question: Explain the JSP implicit objects. List any four implicit objects and their corresponding classes.
questions.hint: Remember that JSP provides 9 implicit objects automatically
keyPoints: Implicit objects are automatically available in JSP pages
request - HttpServletRequest
response - HttpServletResponse
out - JspWriter
session - HttpSession
application - ServletContext
config - ServletConfig
pageContext - PageContext
page - Object (reference to servlet instance)

questions.question: Explain the difference between include directive and include action in JSP.
questions.hint: Think about when the content is included - at translation time vs request time
keyPoints: Include directive: processed at translation time, content merged before compilation
Include action: processed at request time, content included during execution
Directive uses file attribute, action uses page attribute
Dynamic content update: action reflects changes, directive requires recompilation
Parameter passing: action supports parameter passing, directive does not

questions.question: Explain the three types of JSP scripting elements with examples.
questions.hint: Remember the syntax and purpose of each element
keyPoints: Scriptlet: <% code %> - contains Java code executed at request time
Declaration: <%! code %> - declares variables/methods at class level
Expression: <%= expression %> - evaluates and prints result
Example scriptlet: <% int x=10; %>
Example declaration: <%! int count=0; %>
Example expression: <%= new java.util.Date() %>

questions.question: Explain JSP directives with special reference to the page directive and its attributes.
questions.hint: Focus on page directive as it's the most commonly used
keyPoints: Directives provide instructions to the JSP container
Page directive: <%@ page attributes %>
Important attributes: import, contentType, language, extends, isErrorPage, errorPage
import: imports Java packages (e.g., import="java.util.*")
contentType: sets MIME type and character encoding
isErrorPage: marks page as error handling page
errorPage: specifies error handling page

questions.question: Explain the JSP action elements. Differentiate between forward and include actions.
questions.hint: Understand the difference between static and dynamic inclusion
keyPoints: Action elements perform specific actions at request processing time
jsp:forward - forwards request to another page
jsp:include - includes content of another resource
jsp:useBean - creates or locates JavaBean
jsp:setProperty - sets bean property
jsp:getProperty - gets bean property
forward transfers control completely, include returns control back

questions.question: Explain the JSP lifecycle phases in detail with a neat diagram.
questions.hint: Draw a diagram showing all phases and explain each phase
keyPoints: Translation Phase: JSP page parsed and converted to servlet
Compilation Phase: Servlet source code compiled to bytecode
Initialization Phase: jspInit() called once
Request Processing Phase: _jspService() called for each request
Destroy Phase: jspDestroy() called when servlet is unloaded
Diagram showing translation -> compilation -> initialization -> request handling -> destroy
Container manages the lifecycle automatically

questions.question: Explain how JSP works. Describe the step-by-step processing of a JSP request.
questions.hint: Explain from request to response with all intermediate steps
keyPoints: Client sends HTTP request for JSP page
Web container identifies JSP file from URL extension
Container checks if JSP needs translation/compilation
JSP is translated to servlet Java file
Servlet is compiled to .class file
Container loads and instantiates the servlet
jspInit() initializes the servlet
For each request, _jspService() is invoked
Response (HTML) sent back to client
Container may cache the compiled servlet for performance

questions.question: Compare JSP and Servlets. When would you choose one over the other?
questions.hint: Consider separation of concerns and ease of development
keyPoints: Servlets: Java code with HTML embedded, harder for designers
JSP: HTML with Java embedded, easier for presentation
Both compile to servlets internally
Servlets better for business logic and controller functions
JSP better for view/presentation layer
MVC pattern: Servlets as controller, JSP as view
JSP provides implicit objects, Servlets require explicit setup
JSP has built-in tag libraries, Servlets don't

questions.question: Explain the JSP Expression Language (EL) and its operators with examples.
questions.hint: Explain both implicit objects and operators
keyPoints: EL provides simplified syntax for accessing data
Syntax: ${expression}
Implicit objects: pageScope, requestScope, sessionScope, applicationScope
Param, paramValues, header, headerValues, cookie, initParam
Arithmetic operators: +, -, *, / (div), % (mod)
Relational operators: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=, eq, ne, lt, gt, le, ge
Logical operators: && (and), || (or), ! (not)
Empty operator: ${empty variable} checks null or empty
Example: ${sessionScope.user.name}

questions.question: Design a JSP page to create a simple login form and validate user credentials. Show code for both the form page and the processing page.
questions.hint: Include proper HTML form and Java processing logic
keyPoints: Login form with username and password fields
Form action points to processing JSP
POST method for security
Use request.getParameter() to get values
Compare with hardcoded or database values
Use session to maintain login state
Redirect to success or failure page
Code should handle null values
Use jsp:forward for navigation

questions.question: Write a JSP program to display the current date and time, and also count the number of times the page has been accessed using a session.
questions.hint: Remember to use page directive for importing required classes
keyPoints: Use new java.util.Date() to get current date/time
Use session object to store access count
Check if count exists in session, initialize if null
Increment count on each access
Display count using expression <%= %>
Import java.util.Date package using page directive
Output should show formatted date and access count

questions.question: Create a JSP page that uses jsp:useBean to instantiate a JavaBean and demonstrates setProperty and getProperty actions.
keyPoints: Define a simple JavaBean class with properties
JavaBean must have no-arg constructor
Bean should have getter and setter methods
Use jsp:useBean with id and class attributes
Use jsp:setProperty to set bean property from request parameter
Use jsp:getProperty to display bean property
Alternative: use property="*" to auto-populate bean
Code should show proper syntax

questions.question: Explain the purpose of error handling in JSP. How do you create an error page in JSP?
questions.hint: Focus on page directive attributes for error handling
keyPoints: Error handling handles runtime exceptions gracefully
errorPage attribute in page directive specifies error page
isErrorPage attribute marks page as error page
exception implicit object available in error page
web.xml can configure error pages globally
Provides better user experience than showing stack trace
Example: <%@ page errorPage="error.jsp" %>

questions.question: What is the difference between pageContext and application implicit objects? Explain with use cases.
questions.hint: Think about the scope and accessibility of each object
keyPoints: pageContext: scope limited to current page
application: scope for entire web application
pageContext provides access to all other implicit objects
application used for application-wide configuration
pageContext can forward requests internally
application used for storing application-wide counters
session scope can be accessed via pageContext
Both are ServletContext but with different scopes
